segments.text: Asymmetric collapse, asymmetric collapse with straight-ahead flight.
With asymmetric collapse, like with various other collapses, it's the case that, when
we collapse one side, it brakes the wing — so remember,
pulling ears, everything that collapses brakes; in this case it brakes the right side considerably,
and a movement to the right would actually result.
So the wing would happily build up dynamics to the right — we don't want that.
We want, we want with that collapse to (unclear)
of which side.
And then we want to slow that down with the counter-brake. Why with the counter-brake — I'll explain right away.
In theory, it's like this — you also have to tick this off, if the question comes up in flight practice.
To stabilise the asymmetric collapse, you additionally have to put body weight onto this open side and brake a bit less with it.
But in practice it's like this: with a 50% collapse maybe that still works.
With 60–70% — which really is more of the problem for us — that doesn't work any more.
That means, you have so much load gone from the right riser. You can't manage to lean over to the left side at all.
And it's not even productive. If we let the weight fall onto the collapsed side,
then the riser is first loaded again on that side.
That means we help the wing much sooner to re-open.
So, letting the weight fall — you can also remember this for flying.
Letting body weight fall is always good — you always load exactly the side that needs to be loaded right then.
